perturbation.original_text: that any notices under this agreement shall be sent by first-class prepaid post to the address of the recipient party as set out in this letter and shall be deemed to have been received on the second week day (that is, any day excluding Sundays or public or bank holidays in England) following the day on which it was posted.
perturbation.changed_text: that any notices under this agreement shall be sent by first-class prepaid post to the address of the recipient party as set out in this letter and shall be deemed to have been received on the seventh week day (that is, any day excluding Sundays or public or bank holidays in England) following the day on which it was posted.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the notification period to seven weekdays might conflict with the UK's requirements for prompt notice in commercial transactions, particularly if it prejudices a party's ability to respond to legal deadlines or contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Companies Act 2006 (sections concerning service of notice)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Companies Act doesn't specify a precise timeframe for all notices, Section 1147(3) states that notices served by a company are deemed delivered 48 hours after posting by first class post. Seven week days greatly exceeds this deemed period and could cause issues relating to when the notice is effectively served and if a party is acting lawfully upon this notice.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that notices are deemed received on the second weekday after posting. The changed text extends this to the seventh weekday. The Law Explanation references Section 1147(3) of the Companies Act 2006, which deems notices served by a company as delivered 48 hours (two days) after posting by first class post. Seven weekdays significantly exceeds this 48-hour period, creating a contradiction with the implied requirement for prompt notice, specifically two days as stated in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation says the modification 'might conflict with the UK's requirements for prompt notice in commercial transactions'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Companies Act 2006, which supports the Law Explanation's legal foundation. The increased notification period introduces a contradiction with legal expectations for timely communication.

perturbation.original_text: that the provisions of this agreement shall last for the duration of the discussions relating to the Proposed Transaction and for 2 years following their conclusion.
perturbation.changed_text: that the provisions of this agreement shall last for the duration of the discussions relating to the Proposed Transaction and for 10 years following their conclusion.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 10 years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under English law, especially if the information becomes outdated or publicly available. This may depend on the nature of the confidential information, but is generally disfavored as it may restrict competition unreasonably.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restraint of Trade Doctrine (common law principle)
perturbation.law_citation: Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Harper's Garage (Stourport) Ltd [1968] AC 269
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restraint of Trade doctrine prevents contracts that unduly restrict a party's freedom to trade. While confidentiality agreements are generally acceptable, a ten-year period may be considered an unreasonable restraint, especially if the confidential information loses its value over time or the restriction unduly limits future business opportunities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Esso Petroleum v Harper’s Garage | LawTeacher.net Loading Disclaimer: This work was produced by one of our expert legal writers, as a learning aid to help law students with their studies. View full disclaimer Any opinions, findings, conclusions, or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the authors and do not reflect the views of LawTeacher.net. Any information contained in this case summary does not constitute legal advice and should be treated as educational content only. Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Harper’s Garage (Stourport) Ltd [1968] AC 269 Tie in agreement for 21 years was an unreasonable restraint on trade Facts The appellant entered into two agreements for the supply of motor fuel to the respondents. The respondents agreed to a tie-in agreement not to resell motor fuels except in accordance with the appellants’ retail schedule prices, not to operate any discount scheme and to keep their garages open for reasonable hours. The period of agreement at one garage was 4 years and 5 months and at the other garage there was a solus agreement for 21 years and a mortgage with a tie covenant which forbade redemption for 21 years. Issues Thereafter, the appellants wrote to the respondents stating that they would not insist on the implementation of the resale price maintenance clauses in the contracts. The respondents replied that they deemed the agreements null and void by virtue of the removal of these clauses. The respondents began to sell another brand of petrol. The appellants sought injunctions. At first instance, judgment was given for the appellants. The respondents were successful in the Court of Appeal. Decision/Outcome The House of Lords held that the doctrine of restraint of trade applied to both garages. It was noted that the existence of a mortgage did not exclude the doctrine of restraint of trade. The shorter period of 4 years and 5 months was valid, so that the tie-in agreement. However, the longer period of 21 years went beyond any period for which developments were reasonably foreseeable and in the absence of evidence of some advantage to the appellants for which a shorter period would not be adequate, the agreement was void. Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Cite This Work To export a reference to this article please select a referencing stye below: OSCOLA APA MLA MLA-7 Harvard Vancouver Wikipedia All Answers ltd, 'Esso Petroleum v Harper’s Garage' (Lawteacher.net, July 2025) <https://www.lawteacher.net/cases/esso-petroleum-v-harpers-garage.php?vref=1> accessed 13
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends a confidentiality agreement to 10 years, which, according to the provided explanations, could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under English law. The Esso Petroleum v Harper’s Garage case, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 2, demonstrates that a 21-year tie-in agreement was deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade. Although the snippet finds a 4-year and 5-month agreement to be valid, the changed text in the agreement goes significantly beyond this validated timeframe, suggesting that a 10-year period, like the invalidated 21-year one, may also be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the perturbation explanation that the information may become outdated or publicly available, restricting competition unreasonably.

perturbation.original_text: that this agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales and the courts of England and Wales shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims)
perturbation.changed_text: that this agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of Scotland and the courts of Scotland shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims)
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Scottish law when the business, parties, and performance are based in England and Wales could cause conflict of laws issues and potentially invalidate certain clauses if they are interpreted differently under Scottish law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws Principles (Private International Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law rules on choice of law in contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Although parties generally have the right to choose the governing law, the choice must have a reasonable connection to the contract. Applying Scottish law to a contract with no connection to Scotland is unlikely to be upheld and may create uncertainty regarding the contract's interpretation and validity. The first link discusses jurisdiction and procedure for sheriff courts. The second link discusses how applicable law is chosen for contracts, in this example, it is between an English or Swiss court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: North West Lancashire Health Authority v A & Ors [1999] EWCA Civ 2022 (29 July 1999) [ Home ] [ Databases ] [ World Law ] [ Multidatabase Search ] [ Help ] [ Feedback ] [ DONATE ] England and Wales Court of Appeal (Civil Division) Decisions You are here: BAILII >> Databases >> England and Wales Court of Appeal (Civil Division) Decisions >> North West Lancashire Health Authority v A & Ors [1999] EWCA Civ 2022 (29 July 1999) URL: https://www.bailii.org/ew/cases/EWCA/Civ/1999/2022.html Cite as: [1999] Lloyd's Rep Med 399, [2000] 1 WLR 977, [1999] EWCA Civ 2022, [1999] Lloyds Rep Med 399, (1999) 2 CCL Rep 419, [2000] WLR 977, [2000] 2 FCR 525, (2000) 53 BMLR 148 [ New search ] [ Context ] [ View without highlighting ] [ Printable RTF version ] [Buy ICLR report: [2000] 1 WLR 977 ] [ Help ] IN THE SUPREME COURT OF JUDICATURE QBCOF 1999/0226/4 IN THE COURT OF APPEAL (CIVIL DIVISION) QBCOF 1999/0228/4 ON APPEAL FROM THE QUEEN'S BENCH DIVISION QBCOF 1999/0230/4 ( MR JUSTICE HIDDEN ) Royal Courts of Justice Strand London WC2 Thursday 29th July 1999 B e f o r e: LORD JUSTICE AULD LORD JUSTICE BUXTON LORD JUSTICE MAY - - - - - - BETWEEN: NORTH WEST LANCASHIRE HEALTH AUTHORITY Appellant AND: A, D & G Respondents - - - - - - (Transcript of the handed down judgment of Smith Bernal Reporting Limited, 180 Fleet Street, London EC4A 2HD Tel: 0171 421 4040 Official Shorthand Writers to the Court) - - - - - - MR D PANNICK QC and MR G CLARKE (Instructed by Hill Dickinson, Liverpool) appeared on behalf of the Appellant MR N BLACK QC and MISS S HARRISON (Instructed by Tyndallwoods, Birmingham) appeared on behalf of the Respondent - - - - - - J U D G M E N T ( As approved by the Court ) - - - - - - © Crown Copyright LORD JUSTICE AULD: These are appeals by the North West Lancashire Health Authority against the order of Hidden J. on 21 December 1998 quashing its decisions refusing to fund gender reassignment surgery for A, D and G and its policy on which it based those decisions. A, D and G suffer from an illness called "gender identity dysphoria", commonly known as transsexualism. Each was born with male physical characteristics, but psychologically has a female sexual identity. Each has been living as a woman for some years. At the material time A and G had each been diagnosed by a specialist consultant to have a clinical need for surgery substituting female for male characteristics, a procedure known as "gender re-assignment surgery. D was awaiting assessment of suitability for such surgery. They all challenge the Authority's refusal to fund their treatment, including surgery, under the National Health Service because of its policy not to do so in the absence of "overriding clinical need" or other exceptional circumstances. They maintain that they are ill and that the Authority's policy, and refusals pursuant to it, to fund treatment for them are irrational. The Authority justifies its policy and refusals on the ground that it has a statutory obligation to care for all within its area and limited financial resources with which to do so, requiring it to give a lower priority to some medical conditions than to others and that transsexualism rightly has a low priority. Before I turn to the Authority's policy and put it in the context of its statutory duty to provide medical care for all within its area, I should say something about the facilities for and scheme of treatment of persons seeking this sort of treatment. I take this from the unchallenged evidence of a number of leading experts on the treatment of transsexuals put on behalf of the respondents before Hidden J., and which he accepted. There is only one specialist clinic in the country, the Gender Identity Clinic in the Charing Cross Hospital in London. Patients suspected of suffering from transsexualism are normally referred to the Clinic for diagnosis and appropriate treatment from all over the country. The starting point for anyone seeking treatment is a period of consultation with a speciali
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that the law of England and Wales governs the agreement, and the courts of England and Wales have exclusive jurisdiction. The changed text replaces this with the law of Scotland and the courts of Scotland. The Law Explanation states that parties have the right to choose governing law, but the choice must have a reasonable connection to the contract. Here, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that the business, parties, and performance are based in England and Wales, which would give England and Wales a reasonable connection. Therefore, changing the governing law to Scotland, which has no apparent connection, is unlikely to be upheld and creates uncertainty, thus creating a contradiction. The provided snippet does not directly mention this reasonable connection standard, but the Law Explanation does, which is to be taken into account.

perturbation.original_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: G) to destroy 30 days after our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the destruction of confidential information to 30 days after a written request, without specifying security measures during that period, increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, potentially breaching data protection laws like GDPR if the information includes personal data. The original NDA demands it be done 'immediately'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(f) of GDPR requires personal data to be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security, including protection against unauthorized or unlawful processing, accidental loss, destruction, or damage. A 30-day delay in destruction, without adequate security measures, may violate this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 30-day delay in destroying confidential information, contradicting the GDPR's requirement for ensuring the security of personal data. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that personal data must be 'processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage'. The original contract mandates immediate destruction, while the changed contract allows a 30-day window, increasing the risk of unauthorized access or accidental loss, which directly undermines the security requirement of the GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: E) to take all proper and reasonable measures to ensure the confidentiality and security of the Confidential Information at all times.
perturbation.changed_text: E) to take measures deemed reasonable by the recipient party to ensure the confidentiality and security of the Confidential Information at all times.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to allow the *recipient* of the confidential information to determine what constitutes 'reasonable measures' creates a conflict of interest and undermines the protection afforded to the disclosing party. It enables a scenario where insufficient or negligent safeguards are deemed acceptable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith (common law principle)
perturbation.law_citation: Mid Essex Hospital Services NHS Trust v Compass Group UK and Ireland Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 200
perturbation.law_explanation: The Implied Duty of Good Faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. Allowing the recipient of confidential information to unilaterally decide what constitutes reasonable security measures could be seen as acting in bad faith, particularly if those measures are inadequate and lead to disclosure. The first link provides the legal background. The second link provides a summary and legal commentary on this issue.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the party to 'take all proper and reasonable measures,' while the changed text allows the recipient of confidential information to determine what constitutes reasonable measures. This creates a conflict of interest and undermines the protection of the disclosing party because the recipient may deem insufficient safeguards acceptable. The provided law snippet from the Interpretation Act 1978 generally discusses the interpretation of laws but does not directly address the specifics of contractual obligations or standards of good faith. However, based on the law explanation provided, the Implied Duty of Good Faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably, and allowing the recipient to unilaterally decide what is 'reasonable' security measures creates an inconsistency, which falls under the definition of inconsistencies-legal contradiction, because a contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law. This makes the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.
